author: @grendel1960a
text: I purchased the creality ferret pro 3d scanner, this needs a wifi link to my tablet, but I did manage today while out on a bike ride to capture a carving off of a picnic table alongside the stour between canterbury and chartham. I must say, its not in the same league as the revopoint miraco 3d scanner, but at the price point (under £300 in the black friday sales) it works- once you find the right settings for the object being scanned

author: @bugz000
text: you can repair/trim/rotate that model in two simple steps in the open source software called blender, and export as STL, no need for multiple programs...

replies.author: @bugz000
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering  happy to show you how if you would like to pass the knowledge along

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a kind offer mate - but If you want to do that why not do a video on it yourself and post the video to share the knowledge with everyone?

replies.author: @bugz000
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  well funny you say that, i have made a second channel for blender tutorials and plan to start making short tutorials because through helping many people 1:1 with blender i've noted down the main issues they've had when starting out and my videos will tackle those...
however i am in the process of moving to the states so i'm not in a position to be uploading videos until next year...
the reason i suggest showing blender on this channel is because it's a much more comprehensive program, for one i believe it would be better to get people started using better tools so they have the ability to expand further of their own accord without having to change their entire environment with new software... 
and two, i understand blender is daunting at first but you've actually complicated the process using 3 peices of software in this video, you yourself could simplify your workflow by using it, half of teaching is learning yourself afterall

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i actually used 4 programs the revoscan studio on the scanner, revoscan on the PC, 3D builder and cura - The revoscan studio has to be used as it is part of the scanner software - the only reason i transferred to the pc was for  better screenshot but it is still th same software just pc based - of course a slicing software also has to be used - so using blender wouldn't save any software - I could have used prusa which has a split option so I didn't really need to use any midpoint editing at all - I could have gone straight from camera to slicer if I had wanted - but I did want to point out that builder is there and it is a bit like paint - if i want to do a bit of visuals i would use gimp but paint is awesome for screen clips and basic tasks and i do a lot of that so i use paint not gimp for tasks like that. I could have used blender - i actually know blender - but i wanted to highlight 3d builder as being there if folks didn't know about it. So, you are right - there is no need for multiple programs - including blender - it really has no place here. What I find is everyone has a different approach and when you get behind why folks do things it really is often much of a muchness as they say lol - cheers

replies.author: @mobiobione
replies.text: This is a wonderful use of modern technology, to be able to bring historical artwork right into my home from half way around the world in almost real time. Brightens my outlook a little bit, actually, so thank you for these @ThinkingandTinkering  I printed the Lewis Chessmen from Scan The World, over on MyMiniFactory, and some others, like the Olmec Heads, so I'm always on the hunt for more great pieces.

author: @DarienWhitehead
text: I had to restore a set of these! They were very similar to others in palace street I think or a bit further up near the Weatherspoons. The ones I worked on were in Faversham and they were covered in a mysterious substance (I always believed it was the stuff they used to make Osbourne plaques with) that was used to disguise their 'obvious attributes'.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Very cool! thanks for sharing mate

replies.author: @CrossVic10
replies.text: Glad to hear you are still around in Faversham. I still enjoy the St Sebastian- inspired female torso skewered through the chakras that I bought as soon you brought it into the late Alison Baxter’s gallery many moons ago. Mike

replies.author: @DarienWhitehead
replies.text:
